prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response: Victor Jara’s “Te Recuerdo Amanda” sounds like a simple love song—two factory workers stealing five-minute meetings in the hallway, whispering names through the roar of machines—yet every nylon-string strum is haunted by the knowledge that Jara wrote it for the countless Amarillas who will lose their sweethearts to the very factory that pays their rent. When the chorus returns to Espero… / Amanda, te recuerdo, the hope is so fragile it feels like it’s already dissolving in sweat and metal shavings, and that quiet dissolution makes the hair rise on my arms: love is declared exactly at the moment history is preparing to erase it.
